feedback.text: The Stop Destroying Games initiative is currently trying to draw attention to the possibility of stopping the video game service by publishers from a heavier sky. With this comment, I would like to draw attention to this problem here too. Video games that lose their service are not the problem here. The problem is that there are no end-of-service solutions for these games. Video games are marketed as a product but then sold as a service. This should allow an alarm bell, but the problem has not been noticed for years. Video games are sold, but game developers deprive themselves of the right to refuse a game bought at any time. I did not know what should be a ‘service’ on a video game, yet video games are described as a licensed service. It is clear that an online server can offer games to several people together, but what about the possibility of games without such an online server? Single players are a good example, but this term only describes a small part of the problem. For example, why is the replica of a video game and its server prohibited by third lots after the service of the original game has already been terminated by the original publisher? Should the people who have paid for their product no longer be able to use it? That’s just because the developer no longer has any loudness and fails to provide the service? Developers should, however, be able to terminate the service without being able to prohibit the use of the product. For more information, please consult the Stop Destroying Games Initiative or Stop Killing Games initiative. Operators of this movement can certainly be better informed about the problem than I can do. I would also like to recall that this initiative has already gathered the 1.5 million signatures across Europe. This is therefore NOT an irrelevant issue.
